title: Why we fight about the same things
subtitle: The cycle that turns small disagreements into the same argument again
excerpt: Most recurring relationship conflicts are not about the surface issue. They are about unmet bids for connection, old pain, or attachment patterns. This reading explains the cycle and what breaks it.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from attachment research and the Gottman corpus on relationship cycles. No single source. Framing original to homemade.education.

practice.whenToUse: When you notice the same argument cycling, or when a small irritation keeps triggering a large reaction

content.text: Most recurring arguments are not about the thing they are about. The argument about the dishes is rarely about the dishes. The argument about being late is rarely about time. These are surface versions of a deeper conflict, and the reason they repeat is that the deeper conflict stays unaddressed.

Relationship researchers use the term bid to describe a small reach for connection: a comment, a question, a gesture that says I want to feel close to you right now. When bids are missed, ignored, or turned away from, they accumulate into resentment. The argument that erupts later looks like it's about an event. It is usually about a pattern of missed bids.

The cycle runs like this: one person sends a bid. The other person is distracted, exhausted, or occupied. The bid goes unmet. The first person feels invisible. A small event tips the accumulated feeling over. The argument escalates faster than the event warrants. Both people are now defending something, and neither is connecting.

Attachment patterns learned early shape how disagreements unfold. People with an anxious attachment style tend to escalate to get a response. People with an avoidant style tend to withdraw to manage their own distress. When these two patterns meet in an argument, the escalation provokes more withdrawal, which provokes more escalation. The cycle feeds itself.
This is not a character flaw on either side. It is two people doing what they learned to do when they felt unsafe, playing out in adult conflict.

The break happens when one person steps out of the pattern long enough to name what is actually happening. Not the surface issue: the feeling underneath it. Statements like I feel invisible when this happens, or When you go quiet I feel like I've done something wrong, are harder to escalate against than accusations.

This requires someone to slow down before the cycle completes. That is difficult in the heat of the argument. It is more accessible as a practice between arguments: naming what you need before the next version of the same fight arrives.
Drawn from Gottman relationship research and attachment theory. No single source. By homemade.education.
